body:
# Persistent JSON auth + notes REST API (Node ESM, no web framework)

## Context

We need a small self-contained JSON REST API implemented in **plain Node.js
ESM** — no Express/Fastify/Koa or any other external web framework — that
provides username/password authentication and a per-user notes resource,
backed by a **persistent on-disk store** so data survives a process restart.

The service is built on the Node core `http` module. The persistence layer is
an embedded on-disk store: the preferred choice is the built-in
`node:sqlite` `DatabaseSync` API (Node 22.5+, available in this repo's runtime)
opened against a file on disk; if `node:sqlite` is unavailable in the runtime,
fall back to an equivalent single-file on-disk store (a JSON file persisted
via atomic write-and-rename). Either way the store is a real file on disk —
**never in-memory** — so users and notes persist across restarts.

**Authentication model.** Signup stores a user with a server-assigned id and a
password that is **never** stored in plaintext (hash with a per-user random
salt using Node's `crypto` — e.g. `scrypt`). Login verifies the password and
mints a **non-empty opaque session token** (random bytes via `crypto`) that is
persisted and maps back to the user id. Authenticated endpoints read that token
from the `Authorization` header as a **bearer credential**
(`Authorization: Bearer <token>`) and resolve it to the owning user. Sessions
persist on disk alongside users so a token issued before a restart still
authenticates after it.

**Endpoints (all JSON in / JSON out):**

- `POST /signup` — body `{"username": string, "password": string}`. Validates
  both are non-empty strings; persists a new user with a server-assigned id;
  returns **201** `{"id": ..., "username": ...}`. The password MUST NOT appear
  in the response. A duplicate username returns **409**.
- `POST /login` — body `{"username": string, "password": string}`. On a
  correct username+password returns **200** `{"session": <non-empty string>}`.
  A wrong password or unknown username returns **401**.
- `GET /me` — reads the bearer session token from the `Authorization` header;
  returns **200** `{"id": ..., "username": ...}` for the authenticated user, or
  **401** when the credential is missing or invalid.
- `POST /notes` — reads the bearer credential; body `{"title": string,
  "body": string}`; creates a note owned by the authenticated user; returns
  **201** with the created note as JSON; **401** when the credential is missing
  or invalid.
- `GET /notes` — reads the bearer credential; returns **200** with a JSON
  array containing **only the authenticated user's own notes** (never another
  user's); **401** when the credential is missing or invalid.

The server listens on the port from the `PORT` environment variable, defaulting
to **3000**. An npm `start` script launches it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The server is plain Node.js ESM (`"type": "module"`) on the core `http`
      module with **no external web framework** dependency, and starts via
      `npm start`, listening on `process.env.PORT` (default `3000`).
- [ ] Persistence is a **real on-disk store** (an embedded SQLite database file
      via `node:sqlite`, or an equivalent single-file on-disk store) — not
      in-memory — and its file path is configurable/derived so it is stable
      across restarts.
- [ ] `POST /signup` with a valid `{username, password}` (both non-empty
      strings) returns **201** with a JSON body `{"id", "username"}` that does
      **NOT** contain the password (nor any password hash/salt), and a new user
      is persisted with a server-assigned id.
- [ ] `POST /signup` with a `username` that already exists returns **409** and
      does not create a second user.
- [ ] `POST /signup` (and `POST /login`) with a missing or empty
      `username` or `password`, a non-string value, or a non-object / malformed
      JSON body returns **400**.
- [ ] `POST /login` with a correct username+password returns **200** with
      `{"session": <string>}` where the session is a **non-empty opaque
      identifier**; a wrong password OR an unknown username returns **401**.
- [ ] `GET /me` with a valid `Authorization: Bearer <session>` returns **200**
      `{"id", "username"}` for the session's user; a missing, malformed, or
      unknown/invalid credential returns **401**.
- [ ] `POST /notes` with a valid bearer credential and a `{title, body}` payload
      returns **201** with the created note as JSON (owned by the authenticated
      user); a missing or invalid credential returns **401**.
- [ ] `GET /notes` with a valid bearer credential returns **200** with a JSON
      array of **only that user's own notes** — a second user never sees the
      first user's notes; a missing or invalid credential returns **401**.
- [ ] Passwords are never persisted in plaintext (hashed with a per-user random
      salt via `node:crypto`) and never returned in any response.
- [ ] **Persistence survives restart:** a user created and a note added before
      the process is stopped and restarted can still authenticate and read that
      note after the restart (proven by an automated test that reopens the
      store, or by a documented restart check).
- [ ] Automated tests (`node --test`, per this repo's `npm test`) cover the
      happy paths and each error status (400/401/409), including the
      cross-user notes-isolation case, and pass; `npm run typecheck` (node
      `--check`) passes on all authored files.

## Out of Scope

- Any external web framework, ORM, or third-party HTTP/routing/validation
  dependency — the implementation stays on Node core (`http`, `crypto`, and
  the embedded store).
- Token expiry / refresh, logout / session revocation, password reset, email
  verification, and rate limiting.
- Note update (`PUT/PATCH`) and delete (`DELETE`) endpoints, pagination, and
  search — only the create/list surface in the acceptance criteria is required.
- TLS/HTTPS termination, CORS, and reverse-proxy concerns (handled at the
  deployment edge, not in this service).
- Multi-process / concurrent-writer scaling of the on-disk store beyond what a
  single-process embedded store provides.

## Notes

- **Runtime / store choice.** Prefer the built-in `node:sqlite`
  (`DatabaseSync`) opened against a file (e.g. `data/app.db`, path overridable
  via an env var such as `DB_PATH`); it needs no dependency and gives real
  on-disk durability. If the runtime lacks `node:sqlite`, fall back to a
  single-file JSON store written atomically (write temp + `fs.rename`) so a
  crash mid-write cannot corrupt it. Keep the store behind a small module
  interface (`createUser`, `findUserByUsername`, `createSession`,
  `findUserBySession`, `createNote`, `listNotesByUser`) so the choice is
  swappable and directly unit-testable.
- **Schema.** `users(id, username UNIQUE, password_hash, salt)`;
  `sessions(token, user_id)`; `notes(id, user_id, title, body[, created_at])`.
  Enforce username uniqueness at the store layer (SQLite `UNIQUE` constraint or
  an explicit existence check) so the **409** path is race-safe.
- **Request handling.** Parse the JSON body defensively with a size cap and a
  `try/catch` so malformed JSON maps to **400**, not a 500. Centralize a
  `sendJson(res, status, obj)` helper and a bearer-token extractor that
  tolerates a missing header and the `Bearer ` prefix.
- **Data-store file** lives under the repo (e.g. `data/`) and MUST be
  git-ignored — it is runtime state, not source. Keep it out of `temp/` since
  `temp/` is for scratch, not runtime persistence; add a `.gitignore` entry.
- **Verification.** Author `node --test` suites at two tiers: unit tests
  against the store module (including a reopen-the-file test that proves
  restart survival) and HTTP-level tests that boot the server on an ephemeral
  port (`PORT=0`) and exercise every endpoint and status code, using a
  temporary DB file per test run. This satisfies the security-baseline rule
  (no plaintext secrets, validated input) and the testing-standards pyramid.
- Downstream: deliver with `/deliver <storyId>`.
title: Persistent JSON auth + notes REST API (Node ESM, no web framework)
